role_hints.role: Bench and field testing staff (inspectors, testers, samplers)
role_hints.oews_basis: 51-9061 Inspectors, Testers, Sorters, Samplers & Weighers 9.2% of wage bill + 19-4042 Environmental Science & Protection Technicians 1.9% + field sampling/materials techs in the unlisted tail, est. ~10-15% more
role_hints.note: Physically running tests, collecting samples, operating instruments — the core product is a physical act. Lab automation and robotics compress it slowly; LLM-class AI does not replace it. AI-exposed slice is limited to their data entry and worksheet documentation.
Method development, result review and sign-off persist (accreditation requires qualified reviewers); data reduction, QC flagging and report narrative drafting automate substantially.

role_hints.oews_basis: 17-2112 Industrial Engineers 4.0% + 17-3029 Engineering Technologists & Technicians, All Other 2.3%
role_hints.note: Test-protocol design and certification engineering judgment persist; calculation packages, compliance matrices and report assembly automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 7.9% + lab directors/quality managers in the tail, est. ~3-5% more
role_hints.note: Client relationships and accreditation accountability (ISO 17025 technical-manager roles) persist; scheduling, capacity planning and internal reporting automate.
Sample tracking, client status reporting and coordination paperwork automate; client-facing escalation persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-6014 Secretaries & Admin Assistants 1.4% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.3% + admin/customer-service tail, est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Sample login, chain-of-custody paperwork, certificate generation, invoicing and LIMS data entry — the most directly automatable cost lines in a lab.

role_hints.oews_basis: unlisted remainder across lab-specific technician occupations (bio techs, materials scientists, calibration techs, couriers/drivers), est. ~25-30%
role_hints.note: Mixed physical/documentation work; allocate between the physical-testing and reporting patterns above rather than applying one fraction.

source_hints.source: ACIL (American Council of Independent Laboratories) member surveys and publications
source_hints.why: The independent-lab trade association — closest source for technology/AI adoption and business benchmarks in commercial testing labs.

source_hints.source: A2LA and ANAB accreditation directories and ISO/IEC 17025 requirements
source_hints.why: Primary documents for the accreditation gate — what human review/sign-off the standard actually requires, which bounds how much AI savings is realizable.

source_hints.source: Public TIC-major filings: Eurofins, SGS, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, Element Materials Technology
source_hints.why: The global testing consolidators disclose acquisition pace, paid multiples ranges and organic margins — anchors exit_mult and shows how consolidated the top already is.

source_hints.source: Capstone Partners (or similar investment-bank) Testing, Inspection & Certification M&A coverage
source_hints.why: Mid-market TIC deal multiples for buy_mult on $1-10M EBITDA labs.

source_hints.source: UES (Universal Engineering Sciences) and Alliance Technical Group acquisition announcements
source_hints.why: Active PE-backed US roll-ups in geotech/construction-materials testing and environmental/emissions testing respectively — direct evidence for active_consolidators.

source_hints.source: ACIL membership and state-level lab licensing/certification rolls (e.g. state environmental lab accreditation programs)
source_hints.why: Independent-lab ownership demographics are not centrally surveyed; licensing rolls give firm counts and longevity as a proxy.

capture_questions: Testing is sold per-test off published fee schedules against accredited, standardized methods (ASTM, EPA, ISO) — when AI cuts the reporting/admin layer, does per-test pricing hold (clients buy accreditation, not hours), or does lab-vs-lab bid competition on large environmental/materials contracts pass the savings straight through? Which pricing channel dominates the target firms' revenue mix?

Who owns the client relationship — the lab (accreditation scope, proximity, turnaround) or specific certifying individuals? Labs look institutionally sticky; verify switching behavior on commodity tests where the nearest accredited lab wins.

Clients cannot self-serve with generic AI — the product is a physical, accredited test — so the bypass risk is different: can clients bring testing in-house with automated bench instruments, and for which test categories (routine QC yes, regulated compliance testing mostly no)?

What fraction of a typical small lab's cost base is actually AI-exposed (reporting, LIMS, scheduling, certificates) versus physical bench/field labor? The labor_share is 0.68 but most of that wage bill is physical work — capture upside is real but bounded; force an explicit split.
terminal_value_question: Paid demand here is about as durable as it gets — regulation and accreditation mandate physical tests that AI cannot perform, so mature AI cuts the operator's admin/reporting costs rather than replacing the product. The sharp question is the opposite of the usual one: with the AI-exposed slice of the cost base bounded (~quarter of wages, not three-quarters), is the automation upside big enough to justify an AI-premium roll-up thesis at all, or is this just a conventional TIC consolidation play whose economics the global majors (Eurofins, SGS, BV, Intertek) already arbitrage?
special_notes: Cost-structure trap: high labor_share (0.6827) does NOT mean high AI exposure — the wage bill is dominated by physical bench and field labor. Score V off the automatable slice (reporting, admin, data reduction, scheduling), not off total labor.

This is one of the most consolidator-dense codes in 54: global TIC majors plus PE-backed US platforms (UES in construction materials/geotech, Alliance Technical in environmental/emissions, others in food and pharma testing) have been rolling up labs for a decade — strong evidence for B and M, but rule 2 applies: none of it supports V or A.

n_band structure: 187 firms, all from the 100-499 employee class — the 20-99 class (749 firms, avg implied EBITDA $0.99M) sits just below the band cutoff, so the practical target pool is materially larger than n_band suggests; note the sensitivity to the assumed 15.65% margin.

Sub-segment heterogeneity: environmental, construction materials/geotech, food, pharma/bioanalytical, electrical/product-safety and calibration labs have different accreditation regimes, margins and consolidation maturity — identify the mix and avoid one-size scoring; construction-materials testing overlaps 541330 (engineering firms with CoMET divisions) in survey data.

Boundary confusion: medical/diagnostic labs are 621511, veterinary labs 541940, R&D labs 5417, engineering firms with in-house labs 541330 — 'lab M&A' data frequently mixes these; confirm scope before using deal comps or market sizes.

EBITDA normalization: labs are capex-heavy relative to other 54 codes (instruments, facilities) — EBITDA multiples flatter labs vs. asset-light services; check maintenance-capex burden before treating quoted multiples as comparable.
